agent_output: {"executive_summary":"The logistics platform needs a unified API gateway layer to centralize authentication, rate limiting, and traffic isolation that are currently scattered inconsistently across individual services. The design introduces a two-gateway topology: an external gateway handling mobile and partner traffic with differentiated auth and rate limiting, and an internal gateway (or service mesh sidecar) for inter-service communication. This eliminates inconsistent auth implementations, enforces rate limits at the edge, and physically isolates internal traffic from external exposure.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"External API Gateway","responsibility":"Single entry point for all external traffic (mobile + partner). Terminates TLS, performs authentication, enforces rate limits, routes to backend services, and handles cross-cutting concerns (logging, correlation IDs, request validation).","boundaries":"Sits in the DMZ / public subnet. Only component reachable from the internet. Never forwards unauthenticated requests to backend services."},{"name":"Mobile Auth Pipeline","responsibility":"JWT-based authentication for mobile consumers. Validates tokens issued by the Identity Provider, extracts user context, enforces 100 req/min per user via token bucket.","boundaries":"Plugin/filter within the External API Gateway. Does not persist state — relies on distributed rate limit store."},{"name":"Partner Auth Pipeline","responsibility":"API key authentication for B2B partners. Validates API keys against the Partner Registry, extracts partner context, enforces 1,000 req/min per partner, tags requests with partner metadata for auditing.","boundaries":"Plugin/filter within the External API Gateway. Separate key namespace from mobile tokens."},{"name":"Rate Limit Service","responsibility":"Centralized sliding-window rate limiting backed by Redis. Exposes a check-and-decrement interface. Supports per-user and per-partner buckets with configurable limits.","boundaries":"Internal-only. Shared by both auth pipelines. Failure mode: fail-open with degraded logging (not fail-closed, to avoid total outage)."},{"name":"Partner Webhook Dispatcher","responsibility":"Reliable webhook delivery to partner endpoints. Accepts domain events from internal services, transforms to partner-specific payloads, delivers with exponential backoff and dead-letter queue.","boundaries":"Internal service. Consumes events from message broker. Has its own persistence for delivery state and retry tracking."},{"name":"Internal Gateway / Service Mesh","responsibility":"Routes internal service-to-service traffic. Enforces mTLS between services, provides circuit breaking, retries, and observability. No external auth — identity is established via mTLS certificates.","boundaries":"Runs on a private subnet with no internet ingress. Physically separated from external gateway at the network level."},{"name":"Identity Provider","responsibility":"Issues and manages JWT tokens for mobile users. Handles login, refresh, revocation. Single source of truth for user identity.","boundaries":"Accessible via the External Gateway for login/refresh endpoints. Token validation keys (JWKS) cached at the gateway."},{"name":"Partner Registry","responsibility":"Stores API keys, partner metadata, rate limit tiers, webhook endpoints, and partner-specific configuration.","boundaries":"Internal service. Gateway queries it for key validation (with aggressive caching). Admin UI for partner onboarding."},{"name":"Backend Services","responsibility":"Core logistics domain services (orders, tracking, routing, inventory, etc.). Stripped of all auth and rate limiting logic — they trust the gateway to have handled it.","boundaries":"Only reachable via Internal Gateway or from External Gateway's trusted proxy network. Never exposed directly."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Mobile App","to":"External API Gateway","contract":"HTTPS REST/JSON with Bearer JWT in Authorization header. Gateway validates JWT signature and expiry before forwarding.","coupling_type":"loose — standard HTTP + JWT, no shared libraries"},{"from":"Partner Systems","to":"External API Gateway","contract":"HTTPS REST/JSON with X-API-Key header. Gateway validates key against Partner Registry, injects X-Partner-ID header downstream.","coupling_type":"loose — API key is opaque credential, no shared runtime"},{"from":"External API Gateway","to":"Rate Limit Service","contract":"Internal gRPC: CheckRateLimit(bucket_key, cost) → {allowed: bool, remaining: int, retry_after_ms: int}","coupling_type":"operational coupling — gateway depends on rate limiter availability, mitigated by fail-open"},{"from":"External API Gateway","to":"Backend Services","contract":"HTTP/gRPC with enriched headers: X-User-ID, X-Partner-ID, X-Request-ID, X-Auth-Method. Services trust these headers unconditionally.","coupling_type":"contract coupling — services depend on header contract, not on auth implementation"},{"from":"Backend Services","to":"Backend Services","contract":"gRPC or HTTP via Internal Gateway / service mesh sidecar. mTLS for identity. No business auth.","coupling_type":"loose — mesh handles transport concerns transparently"},{"from":"Backend Services","to":"Partner Webhook Dispatcher","contract":"Domain events published to message broker (e.g., order.shipped, delivery.completed). Dispatcher subscribes and transforms.","coupling_type":"event coupling — async, no direct dependency"},{"from":"External API Gateway","to":"Identity Provider","contract":"JWKS endpoint for key rotation. Login/refresh endpoints proxied through gateway.","coupling_type":"loose — standard OIDC/JWKS protocol"}],"data_flow":"External request → TLS termination at External Gateway → Route classification (mobile vs partner based on auth header type) → Auth pipeline (JWT validation or API key lookup) → Rate limit check against Redis → Request enrichment (inject identity headers, correlation ID) → Forward to backend service via private network → Backend processes request → Response flows back through gateway (strips internal headers, adds rate limit headers X-RateLimit-Remaining) → Response to client. For webhooks: backend emits domain event to broker → Webhook Dispatcher consumes → transforms to partner payload → delivers via HTTPS POST with HMAC signature → retries on failure with exponential backoff.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Gateway Routing","rationale":"Centralizes cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, logging) that were previously scattered across services with inconsistent implementations."},{"pattern":"Backends for Frontends (BFF) variant","rationale":"Different auth pipelines for mobile vs partner traffic act as specialized frontends within a single gateway, avoiding the operational cost of separate gateways while maintaining clean separation."},{"pattern":"Token Relay / Identity Propagation","rationale":"Gateway authenticates and then propagates verified identity via trusted headers, so backend services never handle raw credentials."},{"pattern":"Sliding Window Rate Limiting","rationale":"More accurate than fixed windows (avoids burst at window boundaries) and simpler than leaky bucket. Redis-backed for distributed consistency."},{"pattern":"Circuit Breaker (at Internal Gateway)","rationale":"Prevents cascading failures between internal services. Essential when moving from direct calls to mesh-routed traffic."},{"pattern":"Async Webhook Delivery with Dead Letter Queue","rationale":"Decouples partner notification from core request flow. Retries with backoff handle partner endpoint unreliability without blocking the platform."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig (migration strategy)","rationale":"Existing services can be migrated behind the gateway incrementally — route by route — rather than big-bang cutover."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Access Control","classification":"generic","aggregates":["UserSession (entity: session ID, value objects: token claims, expiry)","PartnerCredential (entity: API key, value objects: rate limit tier, partner metadata)"]},{"name":"Traffic Management","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["RateLimitBucket (entity: bucket key, value objects: window, limit, current count)","RouteConfiguration (entity: route path, value objects: upstream target, timeout, retry policy)"]},{"name":"Partner Integration","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["WebhookSubscription (entity: subscription ID, value objects: endpoint URL, event filter, HMAC secret)","DeliveryAttempt (entity: attempt ID, value objects: status, timestamp, response code)"]},{"name":"Logistics Core","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order","Shipment","Route","Inventory — existing domain aggregates, unchanged by gateway introduction"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Access Control","downstream":"Traffic Management","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Access Control provides identity context that Traffic Management uses for per-user/per-partner rate limit bucket keying"},{"upstream":"Logistics Core","downstream":"Partner Integration","relationship":"Published Language — Core publishes domain events (order.shipped, etc.) in a stable schema that Partner Integration consumes for webhook delivery"},{"upstream":"Access Control","downstream":"Logistics Core","relationship":"Conformist — Backend services conform to the identity header contract established by Access Control at the gateway"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Single external gateway with auth pipelines vs. separate gateways per consumer type","gains":"Lower operational complexity — one deployment, one config surface, shared observability. Easier to enforce consistent policies.","sacrifices":"Mobile and partner traffic share failure domain. A bad gateway deploy affects both. Slightly more complex routing logic within the gateway.","alternatives_considered":["Separate mobile gateway and partner gateway — better blast radius isolation but doubles operational burden and config drift risk","Service mesh only (no gateway) — insufficient for external auth concerns"]},{"decision":"Redis-backed centralized rate limiting vs. local in-memory rate limiting","gains":"Accurate distributed rate limiting across multiple gateway instances. Single source of truth for rate state.","sacrifices":"Redis becomes a critical dependency. Adds ~1-2ms latency per request for the rate limit check. Requires Redis HA setup.","alternatives_considered":["Local token bucket per gateway instance — simpler but allows limit * N actual requests where N = gateway instances","Approximate distributed counting via gossip — complex to implement correctly, marginal benefit over Redis"]},{"decision":"Fail-open on rate limiter failure","gains":"Platform stays available if Redis goes down. Users are not blocked by infrastructure failures.","sacrifices":"During Redis outage, rate limits are not enforced — potential for abuse. Must be paired with alerting and secondary protections (e.g., connection limits at load balancer).","alternatives_considered":["Fail-closed — safer from abuse perspective but means Redis outage = total platform outage, unacceptable for a logistics platform"]},{"decision":"Strip auth from backend services entirely","gains":"Eliminates inconsistent auth implementations (the core problem). Services are simpler. Single place to update auth logic.","sacrifices":"Backend services must fully trust the gateway. If network isolation is breached, services are unprotected. Requires strict network policy enforcement.","alternatives_considered":["Defense in depth — services also validate tokens — safer but perpetuates the inconsistency problem and adds latency. Compromise: services can verify the X-Gateway-Signature header as a lightweight trust check."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Auth pipeline plugins","evolution_path":"New consumer types (e.g., IoT devices, admin dashboard) can be added as new auth pipelines without changing existing ones. The pipeline-per-consumer pattern is extensible."},{"area":"Rate limit tiers","evolution_path":"Partner-specific rate limits are stored in Partner Registry, not hardcoded. New tiers or burst policies can be added without gateway redeployment."},{"area":"Webhook event types","evolution_path":"New domain events can be added to the broker topic. Webhook Dispatcher's subscription filtering allows partners to opt into new events without code changes."},{"area":"Gateway technology choice","evolution_path":"The architecture is implementation-agnostic. Can start with Kong/Envoy/AWS API Gateway and swap later. The patterns (auth pipelines, rate limit service, identity propagation) transfer across implementations."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Identity header contract (X-User-ID, X-Partner-ID, X-Request-ID)","reason":"All backend services depend on these headers for authorization decisions. Changing the header names or semantics requires coordinated updates across every service. This is intentional — a stable contract reduces per-service complexity."},{"area":"Network isolation between external and internal traffic","reason":"The security model depends on internal services being unreachable from the internet. This network topology must be enforced at the infrastructure level and cannot be relaxed without redesigning the trust model."},{"area":"Rate limit bucket key format","reason":"Changing how bucket keys are constructed (e.g., per-user vs per-user-per-endpoint) affects all rate limit state. Migration requires careful Redis key migration or a reset."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Gateway becomes a single point of failure for all external traffic","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Deploy gateway in active-active across multiple availability zones. Use health checks and auto-scaling. Keep gateway stateless (rate limit state is in Redis, not in gateway memory). Target 99.99% gateway availability."},{"risk":"Redis rate limit store outage disables rate limiting","severity":"high","mitigation":"Redis Sentinel or Cluster for HA. Fail-open policy with aggressive alerting. Secondary protection via load balancer connection limits. Consider local fallback counters that provide approximate limiting during Redis outage."},{"risk":"Migrating existing services behind gateway causes outages","severity":"high","mitigation":"Use Strangler Fig pattern — migrate one route at a time. Run shadow traffic through gateway before cutting over. Maintain old auth paths temporarily with feature flags during migration."},{"risk":"Partner webhook delivery falls behind during traffic spikes","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Webhook Dispatcher scales horizontally. Dead letter queue prevents data loss. Partner-facing SLA defines delivery latency expectations (e.g., 99% within 30 seconds). Backpressure via queue depth monitoring."},{"risk":"JWKS key rotation causes brief auth failures","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Gateway caches JWKS with TTL and background refresh. Support for multiple active signing keys during rotation window. Standard OIDC key rotation procedure."},{"risk":"Internal services accidentally exposed to external traffic due to misconfiguration","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Network policies enforced at infrastructure level (Kubernetes NetworkPolicy, VPC security groups). Automated compliance checks. Internal gateway binds only to private interfaces. Regular penetration testing."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (weeks 1-3): Deploy gateway infrastructure with passthrough mode — all traffic flows through gateway but auth/rate limiting still handled by services. Validates routing and observability. Phase 2 (weeks 4-6): Enable auth pipelines at gateway for new endpoints first. Deploy Rate Limit Service with Redis. Migrate mobile auth to gateway JWT validation. Phase 3 (weeks 7-9): Migrate partner auth to gateway API key validation. Strip auth code from backend services one by one. Phase 4 (weeks 10-12): Deploy Webhook Dispatcher. Migrate partner webhook delivery from existing ad-hoc implementations. Enable internal service mesh with mTLS.","files_to_create_or_modify":["infra/api-gateway/ — Gateway configuration, route definitions, plugin configs","services/rate-limiter/ — Rate Limit Service (gRPC server + Redis client)","services/webhook-dispatcher/ — Webhook delivery service with retry logic","services/partner-registry/ — Partner metadata and API key management","libs/gateway-contracts/ — Shared header constants and identity types used by backend services","infra/network-policies/ — Kubernetes NetworkPolicy or equivalent for traffic isolation","docs/adr/001-api-gateway-introduction.md — Architecture Decision Record"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use the gateway's native plugin system (e.g., Kong plugins, Envoy filters) for auth and rate limiting rather than custom middleware. Keep the gateway configuration declarative and version-controlled. Use OpenAPI specs as the source of truth for route definitions. Backend services should use a thin middleware/interceptor to extract identity from headers — ship this as a shared library to ensure consistency.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT put business logic in the gateway — it handles only cross-cutting infrastructure concerns","Do NOT allow backend services to accept traffic that bypasses the gateway — enforce at network level, not by convention","Do NOT hardcode rate limits in gateway config — externalize to Rate Limit Service / Partner Registry for runtime changes","Do NOT implement custom auth protocols — use standard JWT/OIDC for mobile and simple API key hashing for partners","Do NOT migrate all services simultaneously — use Strangler Fig pattern for incremental rollout","Do NOT skip the fail-open decision for rate limiting — explicitly choose and document the failure mode","Do NOT cache API keys without a TTL and invalidation mechanism — revoked keys must stop working within minutes"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Two-gateway topology (external vs internal) is well-established industry pattern","Centralized auth at gateway eliminates the stated inconsistency problem","Redis-backed sliding window rate limiting is proven at this scale","Strangler Fig migration strategy is the safest path"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact gateway technology choice depends on existing infrastructure (Kubernetes vs VMs, cloud provider, team expertise) — not specified","Webhook delivery SLA requirements not specified — dispatcher design may need adjustment","Whether 'internal services' includes async workers or only synchronous APIs affects mesh scope"],"assumptions":["The platform runs on infrastructure that supports network-level isolation (VPC, Kubernetes, or equivalent)","Traffic volume is moderate enough that a single Redis cluster handles rate limit state (sub-100K req/s)","The team has or can acquire operational expertise for the chosen gateway technology","Existing services use HTTP or gRPC — no legacy protocols requiring special gateway handling"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"The logistics platform needs a unified API gateway layer to centralize authentication, rate limiting, and traffic isolation that are currently scattered inconsistently across individual services. The design introduces a two-gateway topology: an external gateway handling mobile and partner traffic with differentiated auth and rate limiting, and an internal gateway (or service mesh sidecar) for inter-service communication. This eliminates inconsistent auth implementations, en
